description: Schema version-history audit (the `git log <file>` analog): a read-only command (e.g. `read-schema-history --schemaName <name>`) that prints the schema's history — which migrations ran, when, and from what base — plus the two recording fixes that make the history complete: stamp an applied-at time when a migration runs, and keep a pointer from each new baseline to the one it replaced. Without those two, the command could only show a partial story and "when" would still require digging through git commits: today the only timestamp on a migration decl is its authoring time, and each baseline advance overwrites the prior {content_hash, version} with no parent pointer (the replaced bodies sit unreferenced in the object store). The command composes what the substrate records: the registered migration decls for the schema (from→to, kind, author, authored-at, applied-at), the baseline lineage, and the installed/catalog versions.
acceptance_criteria: A read-only command (e.g. `read-schema-history --schemaName <name>`) prints the schema's history — which migrations ran, when, and from what base; the read path mutates no substrate state.
An applied-at time is stamped when a migration runs against the substrate.
Each new baseline keeps a pointer to the baseline it replaced.
The printed history is complete from substrate data alone — "when" does not require digging through git commits.
Canonical pipeline green (build + check + full test, parity-check, runtime demo of a migrated substrate's history, fresh adversarial probe); docs-surface-sync pass for the new op's surfaces.
notes: Origin: the operator-story think-out — the G2 statement ("As an operator, after a migration has been applied, I want to see the version history of a schema — which migrations ran, when, and from what base — so I can audit how the schema reached its current state. (git log <file> analog.)"). Scope corrected per the status-quo challenge: applied-at + baseline lineage are in-scope recording fixes, not flagged out — they are event metadata, not git refs/DAG machinery, so DEC-0004 does not speak to them.
